coredata.dc:title: Point-of-need quantitation of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid using a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe and a smartphone-based sensing system
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yun
Xiao, Meng
Xu, Ningxia
Yang, Mengsu
Yi, Changqing
coredata.dc:description: This study reports a new ratiometric fluorescence probe (referred to as rQD@PS@bCDs) and a smartphone-based sensing system for on-site quantitation of Fe3+, ascorbic acid (AA), alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D). rQD@PS@bCDs with well-resolved dual emissions is facilely synthesized by covalent conjugation of red-emissive quantum dots (rQDs), the internal standard, with blue-emissive carbon dots (bCDs), the Fe3+-specific fluorescent indicator. 2,4-D can specifically inhibit the enzymatic activity of ALP, leading to suppress the production of AA which can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ and thereafter trigger the fluorescence response of rQD@PS@bCDs. Taking advantages of these highly specific cascade catalytic reactions, ratiometric fluorescence schemes are established for sensing Fe3+, AA, ALP and 2,4-D both in aqueous solution and on paper-based microarray. The smartphone-based fluorescence detector integrated with the rQD@PS@bCDs-based microarray demonstrated good sensing performance with a wide linear range and low detection limit, and is successfully applied to quantitate 2,4-D in cabbage and tap water samples. The reported method which enables point-of-need quantitation in a sample-in-result-out manner and highlights its features of cost-effectiveness and ease-of-use, is ideally for food safety inspection, environment surveillance, health monitoring at point-of-need, especially in low resource settings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Cascade catalytic reactions
Alkaline phosphatase
Ascorbic acid
Fe3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for on-site quantitation of 2,4-D
Ratiometric sensing of Fe3+ using rQD@PS@bCDs
Ratiometric sensing of AA, ALP and 2,4-D using rQD@PS@bCDs
Point-of-need quantitation using rQD@PS@bCDs and smartphone-based sensing system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A-C) TEM images of bCDs (A), rQDs (B) and rQD@PS@bCDs (C). Upper left inset of (A-C) showed the size distribution of bCDs (A), rQDs (B) and rQD@PS@bCDs (C), respectively. Upper right inset of (A, C) showed the high-resolution TEM images of bCDs (A) and enlarged view of TEM images of bCDs surrounded an individual PS-encapsulated rQDs (C). (D-F) The fluorescence spectra (D), XPS spectra (E) and UV–vis spectra (F) of bCDs (blue line), rQDs (red line), and rQDs@PS@bCDsQDs (purple line), respectively. Inset of (D) showed photos of bCDs, rQDs, and rQDs@PS@bCDsQDs under 365 nm UV lamp. Inset of (E) showed the enlarged view of the characteristic peaks corresponding to Cd 3d, Se 3d and Zn 2p. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of bCDs in aqueous solution upon addition of 0–400 μM Fe3+ (λex = 355 nm). (B) Linear plots of fluorescence quenching efficiency of bCDs as a function of the Fe3+ concentration. (C) Fluorescence spectra of rQD@PS@bCDs in aqueous solution upon addition of 0–450 μM Fe3+ (λex = 355 nm). (D) Linear plots of fluorescence intensity ratio I 445 /I 620 as a function of the Fe3+ concentration. The inset photos in (A) and (C) were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(A, B) Fluorescence spectra (A) and linear plots of fluorescence intensity ratio I 445/I 620 (B) of rQD@PS@bCDs upon addition of 0–400 μM AA in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+ (λex = 355 nm). (C, D) Fluorescence spectra (C) and linear plots of fluorescence intensity ratio I 445/I 620 (D) of rQD@PS@bCDs upon addition of 0–160 U L1 ALP in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+ and 1.0 mM AAP (λex = 355 nm). (E, F) Fluorescence spectra (E) and linear plots of fluorescence intensity ratio I445/I620 (F) of rQD@PS@bCDs upon addition of 0–100 mg L−1 2,4-D in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+, 1.0 mM AAP and 100 U L−1 ALP (λex = 355 nm). (G, H) The selectivity of rQD@PS@bCDs to various metal ions (20.0 mg L−1), amino acids (20.0 mg L−1) (G) and pesticides (20.0 mg L−1) (H) in aqueous solution. (I445/I620) and (I455/I620)0 are the fluorescence intensity ratio in the presence and absence of interferents, respectively. The insets in (A), (C), (E), (G) and (H) are the corresponding fluorescence photographs taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

(A, C, E, G) Fluorescence photos of the rQD@PS@bCDs-based microarray taken by a smartphone-based fluorescence detector: (A) fluorescence response of rQD@PS@bCDs on chip upon addition of 0–500 μM Fe3+ (λex = 365 nm); (C) fluorescence response of rQD@PS@bCDs on chip upon addition of 0–600 μM AA in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+ (λex = 365 nm); (E) fluorescence response of rQD@PS@bCDs on chip upon addition of 0–160 U L−1 ALP in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+ and 1.0 mM AAP (λex = 365 nm); (G) fluorescence response of rQD@PS@bCDs on chip upon addition of 0–100 mg L−1 2,4-D in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+, 1.0 mM AAP and 100 U L−1 ALP (λex = 365 nm). (B, D, F, H) The relationships between the R/B ratios of the corresponding fluorescence photos and the concentration of Fe3+ (B), AA (D), ALP (F) and 2,4-D (H), respectively.
Illustration of ratiometric fluorescence sensing schemes for Fe3+, AA, ALP and 2,4-D using the rQD@PS@bCDs-based microarray and a smartphone-based fluorescence detector.
Point-of-need quantitation of 2,4-D in spiked cabbage and tap water samples using a smartphone-based fluorescence detector integrated with the rQD@PS@bCDs-based microarray.

head.ce:title: Point-of-need quantitation of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid using a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe and a smartphone-based sensing system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Sciences, City University of Hong Kong, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Guangdong Provincial Engineering and Technology Center of Advanced and Portable Medical Devices, School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering and Technology Center of Advanced and Portable Medical Devices, School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports a new ratiometric fluorescence probe (referred to as rQD@PS@bCDs) and a smartphone-based sensing system for on-site quantitation of Fe3+, ascorbic acid (AA), alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D). rQD@PS@bCDs with well-resolved dual emissions is facilely synthesized by covalent conjugation of red-emissive quantum dots (rQDs), the internal standard, with blue-emissive carbon dots (bCDs), the Fe3+-specific fluorescent indicator. 2,4-D can specifically inhibit the enzymatic activity of ALP, leading to suppress the production of AA which can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ and thereafter trigger the fluorescence response of rQD@PS@bCDs. Taking advantages of these highly specific cascade catalytic reactions, ratiometric fluorescence schemes are established for sensing Fe3+, AA, ALP and 2,4-D both in aqueous solution and on paper-based microarray. The smartphone-based fluorescence detector integrated with the rQD@PS@bCDs-based microarray demonstrated good sensing performance with a wide linear range and low detection limit, and is successfully applied to quantitate 2,4-D in cabbage and tap water samples. The reported method which enables point-of-need quantitation in a sample-in-result-out manner and highlights its features of cost-effectiveness and ease-of-use, is ideally for food safety inspection, environment surveillance, health monitoring at point-of-need, especially in low resource settings.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticide contamination caused by its improper treatment and abuse, is becoming an escalating threat to public health. 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) which can regulate plant growth by disrupting endocrine activity is a typical organochlorine pesticide and one of top 10 extensively used pesticides worldwide [1,2]. However, 2,4-D is probably carcinogenic to humans, and recent studies revealed that long-term exposure to 2,4-D tends to interfere with endocrine system by stimulating body to secrete a large amount of estrogen, reducing the oxygen-carrying capacity of red-blood cell, causing damage in muscle, liver, kidney, and brain tissue of animals, even irritating eyes, skin, and mucous membranes [3–5]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop rapid and ease-of-use methods for accurate quantitation of 2,4-D at the points of consumption or contamination in support of food safety and environment surveillance.

Highly sensitive and precise quantitation of 2,4-D can be guaranteed by using laboratory-based methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], gas chromatography (GC) [7], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8] mass spectrometry (MS) [9]. However, due to their time-consuming procedure, complicated operation, and requirement of professional personnel as well as sophisticated instrumentation, they are incompetent and insufficient to develop a comprehensive surveillance system for food safety and environment monitoring, especially in low resource settings.

Recently, sensing techniques which utilize biomacromolecules such as antibody, enzyme as well as aptamer and chemical synthetic polymers such as molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) as the recognition elements, have been extensively explored for on-site quantitation of 2,4-D [1,3,10–18]. The antibody-based approaches, for example, immunochromatographic strips, are the most frequently used in-field methods for 2,4-D detection, but usually report qualitative results or semiquantitative analysis [10–13]. More challengingly, high cross-reactivity of most antibodies developed for 2,4-D detection results in poor specificity, because their immunogens are produced by direct coupling to proteins through the carboxyl group in the 2,4-D structure [14,15]. Due to their advantages such as high affinity and specificity to their analytes, low cost and robustness, MIPs have been frequently used as the recognition elements to develop electrochemical and fluorescent sensors for 2,4-D determination [1,3,16–18]. However, MIPs still suffer from their complicated chemical synthesis, small binding capacity, incomplete template removal and irregular material shapes, thus restricting the applications of MIPs-based sensors. In addition, based on monitoring the enzymatic activity in absence and in presence of analytes, the enzyme-based approaches are also frequently explored for sensing 2,4-D [19–22]. For example, a red emissive nanocomposite consisted of carbon dots/cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheet (CDs/CoOOH) is used as an indicator for on-site monitoring of 2,4-D based on its inhibition on the enzymatic activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP). 2,4-D can specifically suppress the generation of ascorbic acid (AA) which is the hydrolysate of ALP and capable of etching the fluorescence quencher CoOOH to turn on the red emissive CDs [22].

Although significant contribution has been made in the development of various methods for 2,4-D detection, it is still compelling to develop simple strategy with high sensitivity and specificity for quantitation of 2,4-D at the points of need in an instrument-free manner. Among all detection methods, fluorescence approaches featured with high sensitivity and high-speed spatial analysis, are believed to be the most effective way to determine and visualize analytes. Especially, ratiometric fluorescence approaches which use the ratio of two emission intensities for analyte quantitation, can significantly improve the accuracy and precision by reducing the interference of external unknown factors through controlling the emission state of the internal standard [23–29].

Herein, in this study, a ratiometric fluorescence strategy is designed for on-site 2,4-D quantitation ( Scheme 1). In the designed ratiometric fluorescence probe (referred to as rQD@PS@bCDs), the polystyrene (PS)-encapsulated red-emissive CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (rQDs) serve as the internal standard, and are covalently conjugated with blue-emissive carbon dots (bCDs) whose emission can be specifically quenched by Fe3+. AA, the hydrolysate of ALP, can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, thus recovering the fluorescence of bCDs and changing the intensity ratio of blue fluorescence to red fluorescence. Based on principle that the enzymatic activity of ALP can be specifically inhibited by 2,4-D, the presence of 2,4-D results in the blockage of AA generation, further changing the fluorescence response of the ratiometric fluorescence probe, rQD@PS@bCDs. In order to ensure portability and simplify end-user operation for on-site quantification, the paper-based microarray chips pre-loaded with reaction reagents are fabricated, and a smartphone assembled with specifically designed peripheral accessories is used to capture fluorescence images of the microarray chips. A self-contained color recognition APP installed in iPhone, Palette Cam APP, is used to extracting RGB values of fluorescence images for data analysis. The reported method is successfully applied for on-site quantitation of 2,4-D in spiked cabbage and tap water samples with high accuracy and precision.

Notably, the following distinctive features highlight the advantages of this study: (i) the large amount of the recognition moieties (bCDs) are covalently conjugated onto the outer surface of an individual PS-encapsulated rQDs, thus providing a stable ratiometric fluorescence probe and enabling immediate as well as highly sensitive detection; (ii) cascade catalytic reactions (Fe3+ and rQD@PS@bCDs, AA and Fe3+, ALP and AAP in the presence and absence of 2,4-D) are conditioned to develop ratiometric fluorescence sensing scheme for Fe3+, AA, ALP and 2,4-D successively; (iii) the smartphone-based portable device integrated with the paper-based microarray chips enables point-of-need quantitation of 2,4-D in a sample-in-result-out manner.

Branched polyethyleneimine (bPEI, MW 600), citric acid (CA), gallic acid (GA), AA , 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyllaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), carbendazim, cyhalothrin, diuron, dodine, methomyl, thiram, diazinon and chlorpyrifos were all purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent. Sodium L-ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP), 2,4-D and ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6 H2O) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology, Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology and Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, respectively. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and urea were acquired from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and ALP were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The surface aminated PS-encapsulated CdSe/ZnS （rQDs） nanospheres were purchased from Shanghai Kundao Biotechnology. All chemicals were of analytical grade and - used without further purification.

The characterization on morphology and size distribution were performed with a JEM-1400 transmission electron microscope (JEOL) and a Nano-ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern), respectively. Surface functionalities characterization was carried out using an ESCALab250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Fisher) and an AVANCE IIIT 600HD Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker). DU730 ultraviolet-visible spectrometer (Beckman) and FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh) were used to record absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra, respectively. The paper-based microarray chips were designed using Microsoft WORD and printed onto filter papers with a ColorQube 8570 printer (Xerox). Quantitation of 2,4-D in spiked cabbage and tap water samples was also performed using a LCQ DECA XP HPLC-MS instrument (Thermo Finnigan).

To prepare the ratiometric fluorescence probe, rQD@PS@bCDs, Fe3+-specific blue-emissive CDs was synthesized at first according to a previously established protocol but changing the reactants [30–32]. In brief, 2.0 g CA, 0.85 g GA and 0.80 g bPEI were dissolved into 30.0 mL deionized water, followed by being heated to 160 °C until a faint yellow solution was formed. Then, 15.0 mL boiling deionized water was added into the mixture and allowed to be heated to 160 °C until a uniform pale-yellow gel was formed. Before the gel was scorched, another 15.0 mL boiling deionized water was added under heating. This procedure was repeated twice until the color of gel changed to orange-yellow. Finally, the product was dissolved into 30.0 mL deionized water, cooled to room temperature to collect the raw bCDs. After being filtered with 0.22 µm Millex-GP syringe-driven filter, the raw bCDs were purified by dialysis tubing with a molecule weight cut of 1000 Da for 48 h. The purified bCDs was then lyophilized to obtain white powders and stored at 4 ℃.

The covalent conjugation of bCDs onto the outer surface of PS-encapsulated rQDs was achieved by the classical carbodiimide chemistry. In brief, 60.0 mg bCDs was dispersed into 6.0 mL PBS buffer with the aid of ultrasound, followed by the successive addition of 0.70 mL of 1.0 mg mL1 EDC and 0.35 mL of 1.0 mg mL−1 NHS. After being magnetically stirred for 2 h under the light proof condition, the mixture was added with 6.0 mL of 0.050 mg mL−1 surface aminated rQDs and allowed to react for another 2 h under gentle shake. Then, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the precipitate was washed with ultrapure water and re-dispersed in 10.0 mL ultrapure water. Finally, the purified ratiometric fluorescence probe, rQD@PS@bCDs, was obtained by lyophilization and stored at 4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for on-site quantitation of 2,4-D
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, in EP tube A, 100 μL 2,4-D with different concentrations (0, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0 mg L−1), 100 μL AAP (3.0 mM) and 100 μL ALP (300 U L−1) were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min under gentle shaking (180 rpm). In EP tube B, 500 μL PBS buffer solution (pH 8.0, 20 mM) containing 0.5 mg mL−1 rQD@PS@bCDs was incubated with 200 μL 1.05 mM Fe3+ for 5 min. Then, the solution in two EP tubes were mixed together and allowed to equilibrate for 5 min in the dark at room temperature. Finally, 5.0 μL the mixture solution was dropped onto a paper-based microarray chip, and thereafter subjected to a home-made smartphone-based reader to capture the fluorescence images under the excitation of 365 nm LED lamp. In this study, an iPhone 12 which equips a 12-megapixel backside-illuminated sensor with 2532 × 1170 pixels (ISO 1250; f: 1.6; 1/17 s), was used for the fluorescence image capture. The collected fluorescence images in HEIF format were then directly analyzed using the Palette Cam APP. To validate the accurate quantitation of 2,4-D using the smartphone-based sensing system, 400 μL the mixture solution was transferred to a cuvette and thereafter subjected to an FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh) to collect fluorescence spectra in parallel.

Since both surface functionalities and emission properties of CDs can be facilely and exquisitely tuned [33–35], blue-emissive bCDs which exhibit an emission wavelength range of 400–600 nm and have abundant surface oxygen-containing groups, were carefully designed and synthesized as the Fe3+-specific fluorescent indicator in this study. A typical TEM image revealed a spherical shape of the as-prepared bCDs with a diameter of ∼5.0 ± 0.5 nm ( Fig. 1 A). An obvious lattice spacing of 0.28 nm was observed in its high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image, revealing the (100) facet of graphene (upper right Inset of Fig. 1 A). The XPS survey spectra confirmed the main components of bCDs were C, N and O, as evidenced by exhibiting the characteristic peaks corresponding to C 1 s (Figure S1A, 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.4 eV), N 1 s (Figure S1B, 399.5 eV and 401.4 eV), and O 1 s (Figure S1C, 531.8 eV and 532.5 eV). The deconvoluted XPS C 1 s spectra of bCDs validated its graphitic sp2 carbon structure by presenting the dominant peak centered at 284.8 eV which is attributed to C-C. The N element dopant of bCDs was validated by the characteristic peaks corresponding to C-N/C-O (286.3 eV) in its deconvoluted XPS C 1 s spectra (Figure S1A) and N-C (399.5 eV) in its deconvoluted XPS N 1 s spectra (Figure S1B). The presences of carboxyl groups on the surface of bCDs was confirmed by the characteristic peaks corresponding to O-CO (288.4 eV) in its deconvoluted XPS C 1 s spectra (Figure S1A) and O-C (531.8 eV) in its deconvoluted XPS O 1 s spectra (Figure S1C). A peak centered at 532.5 eV in its deconvoluted XPS O 1 s spectra (Figure S1C) which is attributed to H-O-C, together with an absorption peak centered at 781 cm−1 in its FTIR spectra (Figure S1D) which is attributed to the out-of-plane vibration of the benzene ring, suggested the presence of surface phenolic hydroxyl groups on bCDs. Obviously, there is abundant surface oxygen-containing groups on bCDs, affording bCDs excellent water solubility and strong affinity towards Fe3+.

It has been well documented that an ideal ratiometric fluorescent probe should exhibit well-resolved dual emissions which have spectral overlap as few as possible and can be excited at the same wavelength [28,29]. Therefore, PS-encapsulated rQDs with a narrow wavelength range of 600–660 nm were carefully selected as the internal standard (red curve of Fig. 1 D). Fig. 1 B revealed that PS-encapsulated rQDs were well-dispersed in water and spherical in shape with a diameter of ∼120.0 ± 10.0 nm, and there was multiple rQDs encapsulated in PS sphere. Synthesis of the designed ratiometric fluorescent probe was achieved by the classical carbodiimide chemistry. Fig. 1 C showed a typical TEM image of the as-prepared ratiometric fluorescent probe, rQD@PS@bCDs, where a huge number of bCDs surrounded an individual PS-encapsulated rQDs. After the conjugation of PS-encapsulated rQDs with bCDs, the resultant rQD@PS@bCDs exhibited an average size of ~140.0 nm (insets of Fig. 1 C). Successful conjugation of PS-encapsulated rQDs with bCDs was indirectly supported by the zeta potential changing from +1.96 mV (rQDs) to − 7.75 mV (rQD@PS@bCDs), since bCDs was negatively charged with a zeta potential of − 13.8 mV.

The successful synthesis of rQD@PS@bCDs was verified by the emission spectra (Fig. 1D), XPS spectra (Fig. 1E) and UV-Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1F). As shown in Fig. 1D, under a 355 nm-wavelength excitation, bCDs exhibited a bright blue emission with a broad wavelength range of 400–600 nm (blue curve of Fig. 1D), while rQDs presented a strong red emission with a narrow wavelength range of 600–660 nm (red curve of Fig. 1D). The emission spectra of rQD@PS@bCDs exhibited the characteristic emission peaks of both bCDs and rQDs (purple curve of Fig. 1D), verifying the successful conjugation of bCDs and rQDs. Evidence for successful synthesis can also be found in the XPS spectra of rQD@PS@bCDs, where the characteristic peaks corresponding to Cd 3d (403.8 eV), Se 3d (62.4 ev) and Zn 2p (1070.7 eV) could only be observed for rQDs (red curve of Fig. 1E) and rQD@PS@bCDs (purple curve of Fig. 1E), but not for bCDs (blue curve of Fig. 1E). In addition, the absorption spectra of rQD@PS@bCDs represented a typical combination of bCDs’ and rQDs’, presenting another evidence for successful synthesis of rQD@PS@bCDs (Fig. 1F).

The as-prepared bCDs exhibited a wavelength-independent emission peak centered at 445 nm (Figure S2A). The strong affinity between oxygen-containing groups and Fe3+ enabled the as-prepared bCDs a promising Fe3+-specific fluorescent indicator [36–38]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm decreased linearly with the increase of the Fe3+ concentration from 1.00 to 400 μM. The quenching behavior of Fe3+ towards bCDs follows Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = 1 + K[Fe3+], where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of bCDs at 445 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. Although a lowest detection limit (LOD) of 0.89 μM was achieved (Fig. 2 B), it is still quite difficult for the bare eye distinguishment of the subtle differences among the fluorescence images of aqueous solution containing bCDs and different amount of Fe3+ (inset of Fig. 2 A). Interference assay results suggested a decent specificity of bCDs towards Fe3+ detection, as evidenced by that most of the coexisting metal ions only induced negligible fluorescence response (Figure S2B).

The absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and the emission spectrum of bCDs presented in Figure S2C revealed no overlap between them, indicating that no fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurred between Fe3+ and bCDs. In addition, the fluorescence decay curves of bCDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ revealed that the fluorescence lifetime of bCDs and bCDs/Fe3+ were 12.7 ns and 11.3 ns, confirming the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between bCDs and Fe3+ (Figure S2D). Herein, it is believed that static quenching should be responsible for Fe3+-triggered fluorescence turn-off of bCDs [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensing of Fe3+ using rQD@PS@bCDs
ce:section.ce:para: PS-encapsulated rQDs exhibited a classical wavelength-independent emission centered at 620 nm with a narrow wavelength range of 600–660 nm (Figure S3A and red curve of Fig. 1 D). More importantly, because of their only negligible response to Fe3+ (Figure S3B), rQDs can serve as an eligible internal standard to prepare ratiometric fluorescent probes for Fe3+ sensing. As designed, the ratiometric fluorescent probe rQD@PS@bCDs exhibited well-resolved dual emissions with only negligible spectral overlap (purple curve of Fig. 1 D). In addition, both the internal standard rQDs and Fe3+-specific indicator bCDs can be excited by the same wavelength (Figure S2A & S3A). As revealed in Figure S4A, the maximum excitation wavelength for bCDs was 355 nm, while the fluorescence intensity of rQDs decreased along with the red-shift of excitation wavelength. Considering that the quantum yield (QY) of bCDs (18.9%) is substantially lower than rQDs QY (~80%), 355 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength for all subsequent experiments.

To facilitate the bare eye distinguishment of the subtle differences among the fluorescence images, the ratio of bCDs and rQDs in the resultant ratiometric fluorescent probe rQD@PS@bCDs was optimized by adjusting the amount of bCDs used for the probe synthesis. As revealed in Figure S4B - 4D, all the ratiometric fluorescent probes exhibited good analytical performance towards Fe3+ sensing, regardless of the ratio of bCDs-to-rQDs ((I445/I620). However, only when the initial ratio of I445/I620 was 4, the stepwise addition of Fe3+ caused the fluorescence images of solution containing 0.5 mg mL−1 rQD@PS@bCDs changed from blue (no Fe3+), purple (80 µM Fe3+) to fuschia (250 µM Fe3+) (Inset of Figure S4C), facilitating the bare eye detection of Fe3+. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescent probe rQD@PS@bCDs with the initial I445/I620 ratio of 4 was used for all subsequent experiments.

Then, the sensing characteristics of rQD@PS@bCDs for Fe3+ quantitation were optimized. Thanks to the large amounts of Fe3+-specific bCDs on the outer surface of an individual PS-encapsulated rQDs, the dramatic change in fluorescence color and the intensity ratio of I 445/I 620 could be observed within 1 min, confirming the rapid response of rQD@PS@bCDs to Fe3+ (Figure S5A). As shown in Figure S5B, bCDs emission was temperature-insensitive in the temperature range of 0–100 ℃, while rQDs emission intensity substantially decreased when the temperature increase from 40 to 100 ℃. This result suggested that the optimal temperature range for Fe3+ quantitation using rQD@PS@bCDs is 0–40 ℃. It has been well-documented that CDs’ emission is always pH-sensitive [36,37,41], the effect of pH on the emission intensities at 445 nm, 620 nm and I 445/I 620 was evaluated. As shown in Figure S5C & 5D, bCDs exhibited strongest emission at pH 5.0, while rQDs emission was pH-insensitive. Therefore, pH 5.0 was selected as the optimal pH value of working solution. Owing to the excellent photostability of bCDs and rQDs, the designed ratiometric fluorescent probe rQD@PS@bCDs exhibited excellent anti-photo-bleaching capability, as evidenced by sustaining ~93% of the original value even after 50 min’s continuous irradiation (Figure S5E).

As designed, Fe3+ triggered fluorescence quench of bCDs while the fluorescence of rQDs were not affected, which resulted in gradual decrease in I 445/I 620 and continuous fluorescence color change of the ratiometric probe solution from blue to fuschia (inset of Fig. 2 C). Compared to inset of Fig. 2 A, only a slightly decrease in fluorescence intensity at 445 nm could lead to a distinguishable color change with respect to the original background, substantially facilitating the bare eye detection of Fe3+. The I 445/I 620 exhibited two linear ranges. One is from 2.00 to 100 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.994 and a LOD of 1.13 μM, the other is from 150 to 450 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.946 (Fig. 2 D). The concentration range of Fe3+ in serum is 7.1630.0 μM and the maximum permitted level of Fe3+ in drinking water is 0.3 mg mL−1 (5.37 μM). Herein, rQD@PS@bCDs can satisfy the requirements of biomedical analysis such as anemia diagnosis and food safety inspection such as on-site quantitation Fe3+ in tap water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensing of AA, ALP and 2,4-D using rQD@PS@bCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As a strong reducing agent, AA is capable of effectively reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+, thereafter inhibiting Fe3+ triggered fluorescence quench of bCDs and changing I 445/I 620 values as well as fluorescence colors of rQD@PS@bCDs (red and green curves of Figure S6). As expected, the Fe3+ triggered fluorescence quench of bCDs was recovered by AA, resulting in fluorescence color change of rQD@PS@bCDs from fuschia to blue (inset of Fig. 3 A) and I 445/I 620 increased linearly with the increase of the AA concentration from 1.00 to 400 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.994. The LOD for AA quantitation was calculated to be 0.66 μM (Fig. 3 B).

Since ALP can specifically hydrolyze AAP into AA in an extremely high efficiency, the ratiometric fluorescence probe rQD@PS@bCDs can be conditioned for sensing ALP or AAP. As shown in Fig. 3 C, in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+ and 1.00 mM AAP, the fluorescence color of rQD@PS@bCDs changed from fuschia to blue along with the increase of ALP concentration, since enzymatic generation AA could effectively recover the Fe3+ triggered fluorescence quench of bCDs. The I 445 /I 620 exhibited a linear range of 2.00–100 U−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.989. The LOD for ALP quantitation was calculated to be 0.40 U L−1 (Fig. 3 D).

It has been well-documented that 2,4-D could inhibit enzymatic generation of AA through specifically inhibiting the catalytic activity of ALP [42,43]. It should be mentioned that only 2,4-D does not change the emission intensity (red and cyan curves of Figure S6) and fluorescence color of rQD@PS@bCDs-Fe3+ composite (inset of Figure S6). Therefore, in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+, 1.00 mM AAP and 100 U L−1 ALP, 2,4-D can restore the Fe3+ triggered fluorescence quench of bCDs, and thereafter results in the decrease in I 445/I 620 value and fluorescence color change of rQD@PS@bCDs from blue to fuschia (Fig. 3 E). The I 445/I 620 decreased linearly with the increase of the 2,4-D concentration from 0.10 to 45.0 mg L−1 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.994. The LOD for 2,4-D quantitation was calculated to be 0.023 mg L−1 (Fig. 3 F). Since the maximum residue limit value of 2,4-D in foods is stipulated to be 0.050 mg kg−1 by China (GB 2763–2021), the reported method could satisfy statutory requirements for 2,4-D quantitation.

Thanks to the high specificity of ALP toward 2,4-D, the reported method is expected to exhibit a high specificity and anti-interference capacity for sensing 2,4-D. In this study, specificity and interference assays were performed by examining the effects of the extensively used pesticides including diuron, carbendazim, cyhalothrin, dodine, methomyl, thiram, diazinon, chlorpyrifos, coexisting metal ions such as Cu2+, Zn2+, Na+, Al3+, Mg2+, K+, Ag+, and other possible coexisting substances such as serine, lysine, arginine, histidine, tyrosinase, on the fluorescence response of rQD@PS@bCDs in the presence of 300 μM Fe3+, 1.00 mM AAP and 100 U L−1 ALP. As expected, only negligible fluorescence change was observed for any examined substances including metal ions, amino acids (Fig. 3 G) and extensively used pesticides, even in the presence of 2,4-D (Fig. 3 H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Point-of-need quantitation using rQD@PS@bCDs and smartphone-based sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to ensure portability and simplify end-user operation for on-site quantification in a high-throughput manner, the established ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform is integrated into a paper-based microarray chip. The paper-based microarray chips were fabricated by printing a microarray pattern designed with Microsoft WORD onto Whatman filter papers using a ColorQube 8570 wax jet printer following a well-established protocol in our lab and elsewhere (Figure S7) [44–46]. Then, the detection zones of the paper-based microarray were pre-loaded with the corresponding reaction reagents, which can be restricted in the detection zones by the wax pattern-created hydrophobic barriers.

In addition, to realize point-of-need quantitation of 2,4-D, a portable device featured with ease-of-use and cost-effectiveness should be developed for precise collection of detection signals generated by the sensing platform. Recently, point-of-need quantitation in low resource settings has been substantially accelerated by smartphone-based sensing systems which are featured with cost-effectiveness, ease-of-use and reliability [44,47–53]. Taking advantages of their powerful built-in sensors, advanced computing capability and user-friendly operation interface, smartphones can simplify the instrument design by simultaneously acting as detector, laptop computers and monitor. Therefore, in this study, following our previous design [44], specifically designed peripheral accessories were assembled onto a smartphone to collect the ratiometric fluorescence signals from the paper-based microarray chips.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, even when rQD@PS@bCDs was coated on the paper-based microarray, Fe3+ could still trigger fluorescence quench of bCDs while did not affect the fluorescence of rQDs. The continuous fluorescence color change from blue to fuschia induced by Fe3+ with different concentrations can be visualized by the bare eyes and captured by the smartphone-based portable device. For on-site quantitation, the fluorescence color change of the paper-based microarray images can be quantitated using a self-contained color recognition APP(Palette Cam APP), which can extract R value, G value and B value of any interested area of images. Figure S8 and Video S1 showed the detailed image processing steps. As shown in Table S1 & Fig. 4 B, the calibration curve exhibited a good linearity between the ratio of B/R and Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 50–500 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.994 and a LOD of 1.89 μM.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132083.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.

Similarly, AA, ALP and 2,4-D can induce the same fluorescence color changes as those observed in aqueous solution, when they were added into the paper-based microarray pre-loaded with corresponding reaction reagents (Fig. 4 C, 4E & 4G). As illustrated in Figs. 4 D, 4 F & 4H, the smartphone-based portable device integrated with the rQD@PS@bCDs-based microarray demonstrated good sensing performance with a wide linear range (AA, 50.0–600 μM (R 2 = 0.998); ALP, 20.0–120 U L−1 (R2 = 0.995); 2,4-D, 1.0–40 mg L−1 (R 2 = 0.994)) and low detection limit (AA, 1.28 μM; ALP, 0.74 U L−1; 2,4-D, 0.062 mg L−1). Although the sensitivity of this method might not be outstanding (Table S2), the reported method could satisfy statutory requirements for on-site quantitation of 2,4-D in low resource settings.

To demonstrate the feasibility for actual applications, on-site quantitation of 2,4-D in spiked samples was carried out using the reported method. To this end, cabbage and tap water spiked with different amount of 2,4-D (0, 0.50, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 mg L−1) were parallelly determined using the reported method and HPLC. As shown in Table 1, recoveries determined by the reported method were in the range of 89.0108.0% with the RSD in the range of 0.45.2%. For the same set of samples, HPLC determination revealed the recoveries in the range of 92.0102.6% with the RSD in the range of 0.5–7.8%. In addition, the paired Student's t-test revealed no systematic difference between the results from the reported method and HPLC determination. Obviously, spiked sample analysis validated that the reported method is reliable and practicable for on-site quantitation of 2,4-D. More importantly, no complicated sample pre-treatment is required, and end-user operation is limited to add samples to the chip, use the smartphone-based portable device to capture the fluorescence images, and open Palette Cam APP for RGB value extraction and further data analysis, enabling point-of-need quantitation in a sample-in-result-out manner and highlighting its feature of ease-of-use. In addition, on-site quantitation of 2,4-D can be realized at ~$0.15 per sample without the use of any expensive equipment, highlighting its feature of cost-effectiveness.

In summary, on-site quantitation of Fe3+, AA, ALP and 2,4-D has been successfully demonstrated using rQD@PS@bCDs-based microarray chip and a smartphone-based fluorescence detector. The ratiometric fluorescence probe rQD@PS@bCDs with well-resolved dual emissions is facilely synthesized by covalently conjugating Fe3+-specific blue-emissive bCDs onto surface of the internal standard red-emissive rQDs, and coated onto filter paper to develop microarray chip for sensing Fe3+, AA, ALP and 2,4-D in a rapid and sensitive manner. Due to the high specificity of rQD@PS@bCDs towards Fe3+ recognition and specific inhibition capacity of 2,4-D towards ALP, quantitation of 2,4-D is achieved within 30 min with outstanding anti-interference capability. A smartphone assembled with specifically designed peripheral accessories and installed with a self-contained color recognition APP, is used for on-site fluorescence signal collection and analysis, providing a stand-alone portable device for point-of-need quantitation of 2,4-D. The smartphone-based device integrated with the rQD@PS@bCDs-based microarray is successfully applied to determine 2,4-D spiked in cabbage and tap water samples. The reported quantitative sensing system featured with portability, cost-effectiveness, ease-of-use and reliability will be definitely useful for food safety inspection, environment surveillance, health monitoring at point-of-need, especially in low resource settings.

Yun Liu: Data Curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft; Meng Xiao: Visualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing; Ningxia Xu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Mengsu Yang: Supervision, Project administration. Changqing Yi: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the Shenzhen Basic Research Program (JCYJ20210324140004013), Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (2021A1515220020) and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments (2020B1212060077) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132083.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yun Liu is a graduate student at School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University. Her research focuses on developing fluorescent nanoprobes and paper-based microarrays for small molecule compound.

Xiao Meng received his M.Sc. degree from the College of Life Science and Technology at Jinan University in 2018. Currently he is a Ph.D. candidate at School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University. His research focuses on functional nanomaterials, biosensors and smartphone-based sensing devices.

Ningxia Xu is a graduate student at School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University. Her research focuses on developing smartphone-based sensing systems for biomedical detection and food safety inspection.

Mengsu Yang is currently Vice-President (Research and Technology), Yeung Kin-Man Chair Professor of Biomedical Sciences, and Directors of the Tung Biomedical Sciences Centre. He obtained his BSc degree from Xiamen University in China, MSc degree from Simon Fraser University and PhD degree from the University of Toronto in Canada, and received postdoctoral training in the Scripps Research Institute in the United States of America (USA). His research interests focus on studying cancer biology and developing biochips and nanotechnology for diagnostics and therapeutic applications.

Changqing Yi graduated from Xiamen University with B.S. and M.S. degrees in analytical chemistry. He received his Ph.D. degree in analytical biochemistry from City University of Hong Kong. He is currently an Associate Professor and Assistant Dean of School of Biomedical Engineering at Sun Yat-Sen University. His research focuses on the development of chemo/bio-sensing technology for biomedical and bioanalytical research and applications.
